name: The Oblique Case
summary: When a postposition follows a noun, the noun changes shape - that changed form is the oblique case.
explanation:
The oblique case is the form a noun, adjective, or pronoun takes when a postposition (में, पर, को, से, का) comes after it. The noun in its plain dictionary form is called the direct case; the moment a postposition attaches, it shifts to the oblique. This is one of the most important mechanics in Hindi grammar, because postpositions are everywhere.

The clearest pattern is with masculine nouns ending in -आ (-aa). कमरा (kamra, room) becomes कमरे (kamre) in the oblique: कमरे में (kamre mein, in the room). Their plural oblique ends in -ओं (-on): कमरों में (kamron mein, in the rooms). Masculine nouns not ending in -aa, and most feminine nouns, do not change in the singular oblique, but they still take -ओं (-on) in the plural oblique, for example घरों में (gharon mein, in the houses).

Pronouns have their own oblique forms that you simply memorise: मैं (main) becomes मुझ (mujh) as in मुझको / मुझे (mujhko/mujhe, to me), and यह (yeh) becomes इस (is) as in इसमें (ismein, in this). Adjectives ending in -आ also shift to -ए before an oblique noun: अच्छा लड़का (achchha ladka) becomes अच्छे लड़के को (achchhe ladke ko, to the good boy).
pattern: noun + postposition triggers oblique: kamra -> kamre mein; plural -> kamron mein

conjugation.other noun singular: घर -> घर में (ghar -> ghar mein, no change)

examples.native: मैं कमरे में हूँ। (main kamre mein hoon.)
examples.en: I am in the room. (kamra becomes kamre)

examples.native: बच्चे बगीचे में खेलते हैं। (bachche bageeche mein khelte hain.)
examples.en: The children play in the garden.

examples.native: हम कमरों को साफ़ करते हैं। (ham kamron ko saaf karte hain.)
यह तोहफ़ा अच्छे लड़के के लिए है। (yeh tohfa achchhe ladke ke liye hai.)
examples.en: This gift is for the good boy.
What is in this? (yeh becomes is)

examples.native: मुझे यह पसंद है। (mujhe yeh pasand hai.)

commonMistakes: Leaving a -aa masculine noun unchanged before a postposition: saying kamra mein instead of kamre mein.
Forgetting the -ओं (-on) ending in the plural oblique: saying gharo mein instead of gharon mein.
Not shifting the adjective: saying achchha ladke ko instead of achchhe ladke ko.
Using the direct pronoun where the oblique is needed: saying main ko instead of mujhe/mujhko.
Applying the -aa to -e change to feminine nouns; feminine singulars usually stay the same.
tip: Whenever a postposition appears, ask: does the noun in front of it end in -aa or is it plural? If so, change its ending first.
